pick.rationale: Alabama holds a commanding statistical edge over Hofstra, ranking 16th in KenPom with the nation's 3rd-ranked offense (AdjO 129.0) and an adjusted efficiency margin of +25.64 compared to Hofstra's +9.46 at 87th, a roughly 16-point gap per 100 possessions. The loss of second-leading scorer Aden Holloway to a felony drug arrest and suspension is a significant blow that weakens Alabama's backcourt depth and three-point shooting, but the Crimson Tide's remaining SEC-tested talent—including Labaron Philon and Latrell Wrightsell Jr.—still vastly outclasses Hofstra's CAA-caliber roster. While the 4/13 seed line historically produces upsets about 20% of the time and Holloway's absence combined with Alabama's early SEC Tournament exit creates real vulnerability, the offensive firepower gap is simply too large for Hofstra to overcome absent an extraordinary shooting performance.

reasoningStep.title: Alabama vs Hofstra – Midwest 4/13 Analysis
reasoningStep.summary: Alabama is a clear favorite despite losing second-leading scorer Aden Holloway to suspension, as their elite offense (3rd nationally) and 16-point efficiency margin advantage over Hofstra should be sufficient to advance.
evidence: KenPom: Alabama #16 (AdjEM +25.64, AdjO 129.0 3rd, AdjD 103.4 67th) vs Hofstra #87 (AdjEM +9.46, AdjO 114.6 89th, AdjD 105.1 96th)
Torvik: Alabama #17 vs Hofstra #92, confirming a roughly 16-point efficiency margin gap

Historical 4/13 upset rate is approximately 20%, but the quality gap here is wider than the typical 4/13 matchup even with Holloway out

result.text: 3 things to know about Alabama basketball's first March Madness opponent Skip to main content Home TuscPreps College Columns TideSports.com SportsbookWire MMA Junkie Golf Week Three things to know about Hofstra, Alabama basketball's first round opponent Emilee Smarr Tuscaloosa News Hofstra is first on deck for Alabama basketball in the 2026 NCAA Tournament. As the Crimson Tide returns for its sixth consecutive March Madness appearance — tying a program best set by former Alabama coach Wimp Sanderson — the Pride is dancing for the first time in 25 years. Here are three things to know about the Coastal Athletic Association conference champions before the Crimson Tide meets Hofstra in Tampa, Florida on Friday, March 20. Ready to win March Madness? Join USA Today's Survivor Pool Today! LOOKING AHEAD: What does Alabama basketball look like without Aden Holloway in March Madness? Nate Oats to face former NBA player on sidelines in NCAA Tournament Hofstra has been coached by NBA former Speedy Claxton since April 2021. Claxton, a Hofstra alum, was drafted by the Philadelphia 76ers in the first round of the 2000 NBA Draft and had a nine-year career in the league as a point guard. Pride will bring international flair to Tampa Four players on the Pride roster bring an international flair. Six-foot-10 forward Victory Onuetu hails from Alhaurin el Grande, Malaga, Spain. Two players — Alex Tsynkevich and German Plotnikov — are from Minsk, Belarus. Seven-foot senior Silas Sunday is originally from Ireland. Previous college home of a former Alabama basketball star Prior to joining Alabama (23-9) for a Final Four run in his final season of eligibility, Aaron Estrada played for Claxton at Hofstra for two seasons. He told the New York Post in 2024 that he had to sway Estrada to enter the portal in order to find an offensive system that would serve him best. "He honestly wanted to stay here, and we had to force him into the transfer portal," Claxton said. "It was hard, because if he came back, I might have had a top-25 team in the country." Emilee Smarr covers Alabama basketball and Crimson Tide athletics for The Tuscaloosa News. She can be reached via email at esmarr@usatodayco.com. result.text: Can Alabama basketball survive March Madness without Aden Holloway? Skip to main content Home TuscPreps College Columns TideSports.com SportsbookWire MMA Junkie Golf Week What does Alabama basketball look like without Aden Holloway in March Madness? Emilee Smarr Tuscaloosa News Spirits were low for Alabama basketball coming into the NCAA Tournament after an early exit from the SEC tourney in Nashville. Now, the Crimson Tide carries more heartbreak to Tampa as it stares down March Madness without its top three-point shooter. On Monday, March 16, Alabama guard Aden Holloway was arrested for first-degree possession of marijuana and failure to affix a tax stamp and was transported to the Tuscaloosa County Jail. The University of Alabama has yet to release a statement on Holloway, or his status. However, facing two felony charges, it is hard to see a world in which the junior returns to competition. Not with the Crimson Tide, but possibly ever again. REQUIRED READING: What failure to affix tax stamp means for Alabama basketball's Aden Holloway GOODBREAD: Why Alabama's Nate Oats should suspend Aden Holloway Holloway's decisions have impacted more than just himself, though. The question is no longer whether Alabama can win a national championship in the face of adversity. Instead, it's if the Crimson Tide can survive even one game without one of the anchors to its nationally-acclaimed backcourt. Prior to his arrest, Holloway was averaging 16.8 points per game. Converting nearly 44% of attempts, he was the nation's 11th best shooter from the arc and ranked third in the SEC. He shot 48% from the field and also had 3.8 assists per game. Alabama has competed without Holloway before — as he dealt with a nagging wrist injury throughout non-conference play. Considering the Crimson Tide's strength-of-schedule, the fact that Alabama has done it without Holloway at 100% could suggest that it isn't time to panic. Well, March is not December. Holloway missed the season opener against North Dakota. He also was sidelined for the SEC/ACC Challenge against Clemson and the next game against UTSA. Arizona marked the Auburn transfer's return, but the 96-75 loss also gave an idea of what Alabama looks like on a Holloway off-day as he went scoreless over 21 minutes. A complete domination of UTSA also made it easier to forget how the Crimson Tide crumbled in the second half against the Tigers. Holloway hasn't missed a game since Dec. 13, and Alabama couldn't have afforded for him to be on the bench against Texas A&M or on the road at Neville Arena. In those games, the 6-foot-1 downhill threat delivered critical buckets late. As of late, he's also emerged as a key rebounder and defender for the Crimson Tide. With or without Holloway, defense has been a problem. Offensively, Alabama doesn't have the depth from the perimeter that Crimson Tide coach Nate Oats had hoped for in the preseason. Alabama finds more ways to score around the rim and from the one-line nowadays. Still, th

The March Madness Bracket is set, and first-round NCAA Tournament matchups are in place. It’s time to make your picks and predictions for the first round of the 2026 NCAA Tournament! We’re here to help as we’ll have picks and predictions for each of the first round 2026 NCAA Tournament games. Here are our NCAA Tournament picks and predictions for Alabama vs. Hofstra. NCAA Tournament Odds NCAA Tournament Pick’Em Contest NCAA Tournament March Madness Bracket Optimizer NCAA Tournament Betting Trends 2026 NCAA Tournament Picks & Predictions: Alabama vs. Hofstra Let’s dive into our top NCAA Tournament picks and predictions for this opening-round matchup. 2026 NCAA Tournament Preview: Alabama vs. Hofstra Alabama 2026 NCAA Tournament Preview Nate Oats has built one of the most explosive offensive teams in the country for a seventh straight year, and the numbers are impressive. Alabama leads the nation in scoring at 91.7 points per game, fires more threes than any team in the country and turns the ball over at the fourth-lowest national clip. The offense ranked third nationally in KenPom adjusted efficiency behind only Illinois and Purdue. The Alabama backcourt is loaded, with Labaron Philon, Aden Holloway and Latrell Wrightsell Jr. all capable of erupting for 20 on any given night, and 6-foot-11 Aiden Sherrell gives the Crimson Tide rim protection. Oats has reached the Final Four and Elite Eight in back-to-back seasons, and it feels like this group is flying under the radar. Alabama finished the SEC regular season on a 9-1 run. Then came Friday night in Nashville. Ole Miss, the SEC’s No. 15 seed, took down Alabama 80-79. The Rebels slowed the pace, ran a disciplined half-court offense, and forced Alabama’s guards into exactly the sort of iso-heavy, careless decisions that fall apart when the threes aren’t dropping. The Crimson Tide shot 31% from deep and had a chance to win at the buzzer, but couldn’t convert on two separate possessions in the final seconds. The loss to Ole Miss was a capsule summary of Alabama’s March ceiling problem. Alabama’s defense is rated 67th nationally. Opponents who refuse to run with them can pick apart the half-court scheme, and Alabama has no real answer when the transition game dries up. result.text: 3 things to know about Alabama basketball's first March Madness opponent Skip to main content Home TuscPreps College Columns TideSports.com SportsbookWire MMA Junkie Golf Week Three things to know about Hofstra, Alabama basketball's first round opponent Emilee Smarr Tuscaloosa News Hofstra is first on deck for Alabama basketball in the 2026 NCAA Tournament. As the Crimson Tide returns for its sixth consecutive March Madness appearance — tying a program best set by former Alabama coach Wimp Sanderson — the Pride is dancing for the first time in 25 years. Here are three things to know about the Coastal Athletic Association conference champions before the Crimson Tide meets Hofstra in Tampa, Florida on Friday, March 20. Ready to win March Madness? Join USA Today's Survivor Pool Today! LOOKING AHEAD: What does Alabama basketball look like without Aden Holloway in March Madness? Nate Oats to face former NBA player on sidelines in NCAA Tournament Hofstra has been coached by NBA former Speedy Claxton since April 2021. Claxton, a Hofstra alum, was drafted by the Philadelphia 76ers in the first round of the 2000 NBA Draft and had a nine-year career in the league as a point guard. Pride will bring international flair to Tampa Four players on the Pride roster bring an international flair. Six-foot-10 forward Victory Onuetu hails from Alhaurin el Grande, Malaga, Spain. Two players — Alex Tsynkevich and German Plotnikov — are from Minsk, Belarus. Seven-foot senior Silas Sunday is originally from Ireland. Previous college home of a former Alabama basketball star Prior to joining Alabama (23-9) for a Final Four run in his final season of eligibility, Aaron Estrada played for Claxton at Hofstra for two seasons. He told the New York Post in 2024 that he had to sway Estrada to enter the portal in order to find an offensive system that would serve him best. "He honestly wanted to stay here, and we had to force him into the transfer portal," Claxton said. "It was hard, because if he came back, I might have had a top-25 team in the country." Emilee Smarr covers Alabama basketball and Crimson Tide athletics for The Tuscaloosa News. She can be reached via email at esmarr@usatodayco.com. result.text: Can Alabama basketball survive March Madness without Aden Holloway? Skip to main content Home TuscPreps College Columns TideSports.com SportsbookWire MMA Junkie Golf Week What does Alabama basketball look like without Aden Holloway in March Madness? Emilee Smarr Tuscaloosa News Spirits were low for Alabama basketball coming into the NCAA Tournament after an early exit from the SEC tourney in Nashville. Now, the Crimson Tide carries more heartbreak to Tampa as it stares down March Madness without its top three-point shooter. On Monday, March 16, Alabama guard Aden Holloway was arrested for first-degree possession of marijuana and failure to affix a tax stamp and was transported to the Tuscaloosa County Jail. The University of Alabama has yet to release a statement on Holloway, or his status. However, facing two felony charges, it is hard to see a world in which the junior returns to competition. Not with the Crimson Tide, but possibly ever again. REQUIRED READING: What failure to affix tax stamp means for Alabama basketball's Aden Holloway GOODBREAD: Why Alabama's Nate Oats should suspend Aden Holloway Holloway's decisions have impacted more than just himself, though. The question is no longer whether Alabama can win a national championship in the face of adversity. Instead, it's if the Crimson Tide can survive even one game without one of the anchors to its nationally-acclaimed backcourt. Prior to his arrest, Holloway was averaging 16.8 points per game. Converting nearly 44% of attempts, he was the nation's 11th best shooter from the arc and ranked third in the SEC. He shot 48% from the field and also had 3.8 assists per game. Alabama has competed without Holloway before — as he dealt with a nagging wrist injury throughout non-conference play. Considering the Crimson Tide's strength-of-schedule, the fact that Alabama has done it without Holloway at 100% could suggest that it isn't time to panic. Well, March is not December. Holloway missed the season opener against North Dakota. He also was sidelined for the SEC/ACC Challenge against Clemson and the next game against UTSA. Arizona marked the Auburn transfer's return, but the 96-75 loss also gave an idea of what Alabama looks like on a Holloway off-day as he went scoreless over 21 minutes. A complete domination of UTSA also made it easier to forget how the Crimson Tide crumbled in the second half against the Tigers. Holloway hasn't missed a game since Dec. 13, and Alabama couldn't have afforded for him to be on the bench against Texas A&M or on the road at Neville Arena. In those games, the 6-foot-1 downhill threat delivered critical buckets late. As of late, he's also emerged as a key rebounder and defender for the Crimson Tide. With or without Holloway, defense has been a problem. Offensively, Alabama doesn't have the depth from the perimeter that Crimson Tide coach Nate Oats had hoped for in the preseason. Alabama finds more ways to score around the rim and from the one-line nowadays. Still, th

NCAA Tournament Odds NCAA Tournament Pick’Em Contest NCAA Tournament March Madness Bracket Optimizer NCAA Tournament Betting Trends 2026 NCAA Tournament Picks & Predictions: Alabama vs. Hofstra Let’s dive into our top NCAA Tournament picks and predictions for this opening-round matchup. 2026 NCAA Tournament Preview: Alabama vs. Hofstra Alabama 2026 NCAA Tournament Preview Nate Oats has built one of the most explosive offensive teams in the country for a seventh straight year, and the numbers are impressive. Alabama leads the nation in scoring at 91.7 points per game, fires more threes than any team in the country and turns the ball over at the fourth-lowest national clip. The offense ranked third nationally in KenPom adjusted efficiency behind only Illinois and Purdue. The Alabama backcourt is loaded, with Labaron Philon, Aden Holloway and Latrell Wrightsell Jr. all capable of erupting for 20 on any given night, and 6-foot-11 Aiden Sherrell gives the Crimson Tide rim protection. Oats has reached the Final Four and Elite Eight in back-to-back seasons, and it feels like this group is flying under the radar. Alabama finished the SEC regular season on a 9-1 run. Then came Friday night in Nashville. Ole Miss, the SEC’s No. 15 seed, took down Alabama 80-79. The Rebels slowed the pace, ran a disciplined half-court offense, and forced Alabama’s guards into exactly the sort of iso-heavy, careless decisions that fall apart when the threes aren’t dropping. The Crimson Tide shot 31% from deep and had a chance to win at the buzzer, but couldn’t convert on two separate possessions in the final seconds. The loss to Ole Miss was a capsule summary of Alabama’s March ceiling problem. Alabama’s defense is rated 67th nationally. Opponents who refuse to run with them can pick apart the half-court scheme, and Alabama has no real answer when the transition game dries up.
